Measure the supply voltages of the processor, the memory and the chipset at the control points of the board.
Check the absence of short circuits in the power circuits by measuring the resistance to ground with the power off.
Observe the clock and control signals with the oscilloscope and assess their shape and amplitude.
Insert a POST card and analyse the codes of the passage of the POST.
Compile a protocol of the measurements and make a conclusion about the state of the electrical circuits of the computer.
tasks: Prepare a protocol of the measurements of the voltages with a comparison with the nominal values.
Describe the procedure for detecting a short circuit in the power circuits.
Make a conclusion about the operability of the electrical circuits of the diagnosed board.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has mastered the methods of the comprehensive diagnostics and measurement of the parameters of the electrical circuits of a computer with the use of a multimeter, an oscilloscope and a POST card.
questions: At which control points are the supply voltages of a computer measured?
How is the absence of short circuits in the power circuits checked?
What does the oscilloscope allow one to assess?
How does a POST card help in the diagnostics?
How are the results of the measurements compared with the nominal values?

lessons.title: Detecting faults in the circuits of the sound subsystem of computers
outline: The structure and operating principle of the sound subsystem.
The signal and power circuits of the sound path.
Typical faults of the sound subsystem.
Methods of diagnosing sound faults.
body: The sound subsystem of a computer provides the input and output of audio signals. It may be integrated into the motherboard (an audio codec) or made as a separate sound card. The understanding of its structure and circuits allows one to diagnose the typical faults of sound.

The basis of the sound subsystem is the audio codec — a microcircuit that converts the digital sound into an analog signal (a DAC) for output to speakers and headphones, and the analog signal into a digital one (an ADC) for input from a microphone. The codec is connected to the chipset over a special interface.

The sound path includes the codec, the analog amplification and filtering circuits, the output connectors (the jacks), the input circuits of the microphone and the line input. The quality of the sound depends on the quality of these circuits and on the screening from the interference of the digital part.

The power supply of the sound subsystem is performed from the supply voltages of the board, often through additional filters to reduce interference. The analog circuits are sensitive to interference, so they are separated from the digital part and screened.

The typical faults of the sound subsystem are: the complete absence of sound, the absence of sound in one channel, noise and crackling, a low volume, and the inoperability of the microphone or the line input. These faults may be caused by the hardware, the drivers or the settings.

Before searching for a hardware fault, one must exclude software causes: check the connection of the speakers, the volume settings, the choice of the playback device, and the presence and operation of the driver of the audio codec. A significant part of the problems of sound is solved at the software level.

The hardware diagnostics of the sound subsystem includes checking the output connectors (the jacks), which often wear out and oxidize, the integrity of the contacts, and the operability of the codec. The faults of the connectors are a frequent cause of the absence of sound in a channel.

The measurement of the signals in the sound path with an oscilloscope allows one to trace where the signal disappears or is distorted. The comparison with a serviceable device and the method of substitution help to localize the faulty node.

Noise and crackling in the sound are often caused by the interference of the digital part, a bad earthing or a fault of the filtering circuits. The improvement of the screening and the earthing, and the elimination of the sources of interference reduce the noise.

If the integrated sound subsystem is faulty and the repair is impossible, the simplest solution is to install a separate sound card or to use an external USB sound adapter, which completely replaces the faulty path.

The diagnostics of the microphone and the line input is performed similarly: the checking of the connectors, the settings, the driver and the signal circuits. The recording and playback of a test signal helps to assess the operability of the input path.

Thus, the detection of faults in the circuits of the sound subsystem combines the exclusion of software causes, the checking of the connectors and the codec, the measurement of the signals and the methods of substitution and comparison, which allows the typical faults of sound to be localized and eliminated.
questions: What does the sound subsystem of a computer consist of, and what function does the audio codec perform?
What is included in the sound path, and why are the analog circuits screened?
What are the typical faults of the sound subsystem?
What software causes of the absence of sound must be excluded before searching for a hardware fault?
How can the operability of a faulty integrated sound subsystem be restored?

lessons.title: Testing and analysis of the circuits of USB and other input/output ports
lessons.objective: To form practical skills in testing and analysing the circuits of USB and other input/output ports and in detecting their faults.
theory: USB ports provide the connection of peripheral devices and their power supply. Each USB port has data lines and power lines (+5 V and ground). The faults of the ports are connected with the connectors, the power circuits and the controllers.

In addition to USB, a computer has other input/output ports: PS/2, the audio jacks, the video outputs, and the network connector. The testing of the ports includes checking the supply voltage, the data exchange and the integrity of the connectors.
materials: A computer with a set of USB and other ports.
Test USB devices (a flash drive, a mouse).
A multimeter and a USB tester.
A report form.
procedure: Examine the USB ports of the computer and check the integrity of the connectors and the absence of mechanical damage.
Measure the supply voltage (+5 V) at the USB ports with a multimeter or a USB tester.
Check the operation of the ports by connecting test devices and assessing their recognition by the system.
Test the data-transmission speed of the USB ports of different versions (2.0, 3.0) using a flash drive.
Check the operation of the other ports (audio, network, video) by connecting the corresponding devices.
Make a conclusion about the operability of the ports and about the detected faults.
tasks: Compile a table of the ports of the computer with the results of testing each of them.
Describe the typical faults of USB ports and the methods of their detection.
Measure and compare the data-transmission speed of USB 2.0 and USB 3.0 ports.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of testing and analysing the circuits of USB and other input/output ports and of detecting their faults.
questions: What lines does a USB port contain, and what voltage is supplied to it?
How is the supply voltage of a USB port checked?
What are the typical faults of USB ports?
How is the data-transmission speed of USB ports tested?
What other input/output ports does a computer have?

lessons.title: Analysis of the circuits of temperature monitoring and control in computers
outline: Temperature sensors and their placement in a computer.
The circuits of monitoring and reading the sensors.
The mechanisms of protection against overheating.
Software monitoring of temperatures.
body: The control of the temperature of the components is critically important for the reliable operation of a computer. Overheating leads to the degradation and failure of components, to throttling and to shutdowns. The circuits of temperature monitoring and control track the heating and protect the equipment.

Temperature sensors are placed in the key nodes of a computer: inside the processor and the graphics processor (built-in digital sensors), on the motherboard near the VRM and the chipset, in the drives (through the S.M.A.R.T.), and sometimes in the case. They measure the temperature in real time.

The built-in sensors of the processor (the DTS — digital thermal sensors) measure the temperature of each core and transmit the data to the registers, from which the operating system and the utilities read them. These sensors are also used by the protection mechanisms of the processor itself.

The motherboard contains a hardware-monitoring controller (the Super I/O or a separate chip) that reads the analog and digital temperature sensors, the voltages and the speed of the fans. This controller provides the data for monitoring and controls the fans.

The reading of the sensors is performed over special buses (for example, I2C/SMBus). The monitoring utilities turn to the hardware-monitoring controller and the registers of the processor and the video card to obtain the values of the temperatures, the voltages and the frequencies.

The mechanisms of protection against overheating operate at several levels. The first level is the regulation of the speed of the fans depending on the temperature. The second level is throttling — the reduction of the frequency and voltage when the threshold temperature is reached. The third level is the emergency shutdown at a critical temperature.

Thermal throttling protects the components from damage but reduces the performance. The constant occurrence of throttling indicates an insufficient efficiency of cooling and requires maintenance — the cleaning of the radiators and the replacement of the thermal paste.

The emergency shutdown (thermal shutdown) occurs when the protection mechanisms cannot keep the temperature within the limits. This is the last line of defence, which prevents the physical destruction of the components. Frequent emergency shutdowns are a serious signal of a problem of cooling.

Software monitoring of temperatures is performed by utilities (HWMonitor, HWiNFO, AIDA64, Core Temp), which display the current, minimum and maximum values of the temperatures of all the nodes. The analysis of these data allows one to assess the state of cooling and to detect the overheating nodes.

The setting of the monitoring includes the choice of the displayed sensors, the setting of the alarm thresholds and the logging of the temperatures over time. The logging is useful for detecting overheating under specific loads and for diagnosing 'floating' problems.

The correct interpretation of the readings of the sensors requires the knowledge of the permissible values for each component. The temperatures at rest and under load are compared with the recommendations of the manufacturers, which allows one to assess whether the cooling system copes with its task.

Thus, the analysis of the circuits of temperature monitoring and control includes the understanding of the placement and operation of the sensors, the circuits of their reading, the multi-level mechanisms of protection against overheating and the software monitoring, which together ensure the thermal safety of a computer.
questions: Where are the temperature sensors placed in a computer?
What controller of the motherboard reads the temperatures, the voltages and the speed of the fans?
What levels of protection against overheating exist?
What does the constant occurrence of thermal throttling indicate?
What utilities are used for the software monitoring of temperatures?

lessons.title: Analysis of the typical faults of the central processor, the random-access memory and the motherboard
lessons.objective: To form practical skills in analysing and localizing the typical faults of the central processor, the random-access memory and the motherboard.
theory: The faults of the processor, the memory and the motherboard often have similar manifestations (the failure to start, freezes, restarts), which complicates the diagnostics. The task of the technician is to localize the fault to a specific node by the method of exclusion and substitution.

The diagnostics is built on the principle of the minimal configuration and the gradual substitution of components: the board, the processor, one memory module and the power supply. The substitution of components for obviously serviceable ones allows one to determine the faulty node.
materials: A set of components (a board, a processor, memory) for diagnostics.
Serviceable components for substitution.
A POST card and a multimeter.
A report form.
procedure: Assemble a minimal configuration and check the start and the passage of the POST.
Use a POST card to determine the stage at which the start stops.
Check the memory by substituting the modules and reseating them in different slots.
Check the processor by inspecting the socket, the contacts and (if possible) by substitution.
Check the power circuits of the board by measuring the supply voltages of the processor and the memory.
Localize the faulty node and make a conclusion about the cause of the fault.
tasks: Compile a table of the typical faults of the processor, the memory and the board with their manifestations.
Describe the procedure for localizing a fault by the method of the minimal configuration.
Make a conclusion about the faulty node in a given case and propose a method of elimination.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has mastered the methods of analysing and localizing the typical faults of the processor, the memory and the motherboard by the methods of the minimal configuration and substitution.
questions: Why do the faults of the processor, the memory and the board have similar manifestations?
What is the method of the minimal configuration?
How is a memory fault localized?
How is the serviceability of the power circuits of the board checked?
How does a POST card help to localize a fault?

lessons.title: The role of circuit engineering in protecting computer systems from overheating and overvoltage
lessons.objective: To form practical skills in analysing the circuit-engineering means of protecting computer systems from overheating and overvoltage and in assessing their operability.
theory: The circuit-engineering protection of computer systems includes the means of protection against overheating (temperature monitoring, throttling, the control of the fans) and against overvoltage (filters, stabilizers, protective diodes, fuses).

The protection against overvoltage in the power supply and on the motherboard prevents the damage of the components in the event of voltage surges and faults. The understanding of these means allows one to assess the reliability of the system and to diagnose the protection circuits.
materials: A power supply and a motherboard for analysis.
A multimeter and an oscilloscope.
A schematic diagram of the protection circuits.
A report form.
procedure: Study the means of protection against overheating in the system: the sensors, the throttling, the control of the fans.
Find the elements of protection against overvoltage in the power supply (the input filter, the fuse, the protective circuits).
Examine the protective elements on the motherboard (the filters, the protective diodes at the power inputs and the ports).
Check the operability of the temperature protection by observing the reaction of the system to a load and heating.
Describe how the protection circuits prevent the damage of the components in the event of faults.
Make a conclusion about the operability of the protection means of the analysed system.
tasks: Compile a list of the circuit-engineering means of protection against overheating and overvoltage.
Describe the operation of the protection against overvoltage in a power supply.
Assess the operability of the temperature protection of the analysed system.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has formed the skills of analysing the circuit-engineering means of protecting computer systems from overheating and overvoltage and of assessing their operability.
questions: What circuit-engineering means protect a computer from overheating?
What elements protect a computer from overvoltage?
How does the protection against overvoltage in a power supply work?
How is the operability of the temperature protection checked?
Why is the protection of the power inputs and the ports important?

lessons.title: Modern methods of diagnosing integrated circuits, microcontrollers and monitoring systems of computers
lessons.objective: To form practical skills in applying modern methods of diagnosing integrated circuits, microcontrollers and monitoring systems of computers.
theory: Modern computer systems are built on integrated circuits and microcontrollers that perform the functions of control and monitoring. Their diagnostics combines hardware methods (the measurement of the power supply, the signals, the use of an oscilloscope) and software methods (reading the data of the sensors, analysing the logs).

Monitoring systems collect data on the state of the equipment (temperatures, voltages, frequencies, the speed of the fans) and provide it to the operating system and the utilities. The analysis of these data is a modern non-destructive method of diagnostics.
materials: A computer with monitoring systems.
Diagnostic utilities (HWiNFO, AIDA64).
An oscilloscope and a multimeter.
A report form.
procedure: Read the data of all the available sensors of the computer with a monitoring utility and analyse them.
Identify the microcontrollers and the monitoring circuits on the motherboard (the Super I/O, the hardware-monitoring controller).
Check the power supply and the clock signals of the microcontrollers with a multimeter and an oscilloscope.
Compare the readings of the software monitoring with the results of the hardware measurements.
Detect deviations of the parameters and localize the possible causes.
Make a conclusion about the state of the integrated circuits and the monitoring systems.
tasks: Prepare a report on the analysis of the data of the monitoring of a computer.
Compare the readings of the software and hardware diagnostics.
Describe the modern non-destructive methods of diagnosing integrated circuits.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has mastered the modern methods of diagnosing integrated circuits, microcontrollers and monitoring systems, combining hardware and software approaches.
questions: On what basis are modern computer systems built?
What hardware and software methods of diagnostics are combined?
What monitoring circuits are located on a motherboard?
How are the readings of the software and hardware diagnostics compared?
What are the advantages of the non-destructive methods of diagnostics?

lessons.title: Diagnostics and repair of integrated circuits and microcontrollers in computers
lessons.objective: To consolidate and check, in a comprehensive form, the skills of diagnosing and repairing integrated circuits and microcontrollers in computers, summing up the learning outcome on circuit engineering.
theory: This lesson generalizes the material of the learning outcome on determining the operability of computer systems at the circuit-engineering level. The student must demonstrate the ability to diagnose comprehensively, from the power supply to the integrated circuits, and to determine the methods of repair.

The repair of integrated circuits and microcontrollers requires special equipment (a soldering and an infrared station for BGA), measuring instruments and skills of working with miniature components. In many cases, the repair is reduced to the replacement of a board or a module.
materials: A motherboard and components for comprehensive diagnostics.
A multimeter, an oscilloscope, a POST card and a soldering station.
A set of situational problems on diagnostics and repair.
A final-assessment form.
procedure: Perform a comprehensive diagnostics of the proposed board: a visual inspection, the checking of the power supply, the clock signals and the POST.
Localize the fault to a specific node or integrated circuit using all the studied methods.
Determine whether the detected fault can be repaired and what equipment is required for this.
Describe the technology of replacing a faulty microcircuit (the preparation, the dismantling, the installation, the verification).
Solve the proposed situational problems on the diagnostics and repair of computer systems.
Make a final conclusion about the state of the board and about the method of restoring its operability.
tasks: Prepare a comprehensive diagnostic report for the proposed board.
Describe the technology of replacing a faulty integrated circuit.
Solve a situational problem on determining the cause of a fault and the method of its elimination.
lessons.conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has demonstrated comprehensive skills of diagnosing and repairing integrated circuits and microcontrollers and the ability to determine the operability of computer systems at the circuit-engineering level.
questions: What does the comprehensive diagnostics of a motherboard include?
How is a fault localized to a specific integrated circuit?
What equipment is required for the repair of microcircuits?
What is the technology of replacing a faulty microcircuit?
In which cases is the repair of an integrated circuit not advisable?
